name: Algebra
description: Groups, rings, fields, number theory. The mathematics of abstract structure and symmetry. Foundation for cryptography, coding theory, Galois theory, formal verification, and the algebraic topology chain that underlies homology and cohomology. Dependencies: root of the math tree — feeds into 500 (calculus via algebraic limits), 533 (analysis via topological groups), 555 (topology via algebraic invariants), 566 (linear algebra via modules over rings), 600 (probability via measure-theoretic foundations), 633 (statistics via group-theoretic experimental design), and 650 (discrete math via finite fields and combinatorics).
axioms: Group axioms: closure, associativity, identity element, inverse element
Ring axioms: two binary operations (+, ×) with additive abelian group + multiplicative monoid + distributivity
Field axioms: commutative ring with multiplicative inverses for non-zero elements
Fundamental theorem of arithmetic: unique prime factorization
Euler's theorem: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) for gcd(a,n)=1 — foundation of RSA cryptosystem
Lagrange's theorem: subgroup order divides group order
Fundamental theorem of Galois theory: field extensions ↔ subgroup lattices
Chinese remainder theorem: simultaneous congruences modulo coprime moduli
reasoning_patterns: representation theory: group actions on vector spaces
boundary_conditions: Not all Diophantine equations are solvable (Hilbert's 10th: undecidable)
Polynomials of degree ≥ 5 have no general radical solution (Abel-Ruffini)
Some algebraic structures are not classifiable (infinite groups)
Fermat's Last Theorem required 350+ years of number theory to prove
